figures.caption: Figure 4. Latency and peak memory at 1K, 4K, and 16K tokens (FP16/FP32). In FP32, ELSA reduces latency over MathSDPA and ME-SDPA while maintaining low memory. In FP16, ELSA achieves the lowest memory among exact kernels and remains competitive with FA2/FA3 at longer sequences; at 16K tokens, standard softmax attention fails with OOM while ELSA remains stable.

Table 9. Vectorized equivalence (oracle-based). 95th-percentile drift metrics against a high-precision (float64) reference, across all batches, heads, and tokens. All three scenarios show nearmachine-precision agreement.

Figure 1. Integral-image analogy and ELSA computational flow. (a) Just as a prefix-sum table [6] enables O(1) range queries by composing boundary values, the softmax state (m,S,W ) supports exact blockwise composition via ⊕ (Proposition 1). (b) ME-SDPA [5] chains tokens sequentially via the online softmax recurrence [29], incurring O(n) depth that becomes prohibitive at long sequences. (c) ELSA reduces depth to O(logn) via a two-level prefix scan: Hillis–Steele [16] within blocks (all threads active, maximizing sharedmemory bandwidth) and Blelloch [1] across blocks (work-optimal, minimizing global memory traffic)—while preserving exact FP32 semantics, O(n) memory, and hardware agnosticism: ⊕ requires only scalar max/exp and vector fma, with no Tensor Core dependency.

Table 11. VGGT baseline. ELSA-FP32 achieves 1.46–2.34× speedup over xFormers-FP32 while matching its memory footprint. Speedup is relative to xFormers-FP32; shading intensity reflects speedup magnitude.

Table 12. FastVGGT scaling. ELSA-FP32 sustains 1.18–1.38× speedup at long sequences with identical memory overhead. Math baselines fail beyond 30 frames (OOM); shading intensity reflects speedup magnitude, red denotes below-baseline performance.
